title: code-index alive-but-stale reclaim spawns a second daemon without reaping the live orphan

findingDetails.evidence: The staleReclaimable branch (979-981) is only `log('staleReclaimed: true')`, then control falls through to acquireBootstrapLock (986) → writeLeaseFile (1054) → launchServer() (1061), which spawns a fresh daemon. The daemon lease writes only `pid: process.pid` with no childPid (writeLeaseFile, 705-710), so the orphan daemon pid is never recorded or checked. acquireOwnerLeaseFile reclaims a `ppid-1-orphan` owner lease by overwriting it (450-486) with no process reap. By contrast mk-spec-memory-launcher.cjs:1570-1652 deep-probes and adopts-or-reaps the recorded childPid under a respawn lock, and mk-skill-advisor-launcher.cjs:731-760 bridges or scheduleBootstrapReap's the orphan inside the bootstrap-lock section.
findingDetails.why: When a code-index launcher is SIGKILLed, its non-detached child (launchServer, 875, no detached/unref) is reparented to init and keeps running while holding code-graph.sqlite. The next launcher classifies the orphan owner lease as ppid-1-orphan (reclaimable) and the daemon lease as staleReclaimable, then spawns a SECOND daemon against the same SQLite DB without reaping the first — the exact double-spawn the other two launchers guard against. Impact is bounded only by the code-index daemon's own DB lock (outside this slice); at minimum it leaks an orphan and risks contended/double writers.
findingDetails.recommendation: Mirror the sibling launchers: record the daemon childPid in code-index's lease, and in the staleReclaimable branch (and the ppid-1-orphan owner-lease reclaim) adopt the orphan via a deep probe when bridgeable, else reap it (confirming exit) under a serialization lock before launchServer(). If the no-reap behavior is intentional (daemon DB-lock backstop), add a comment citing that guarantee, since its absence reads as an oversight relative to the two siblings.
